title: Fantasy Road Encounters
description: What travelers find on the roads between settlements in a world where the wilderness is genuinely wild — pilgrims, merchants, strange weather, old magic, and the full spectrum of humanity moving through a world that has its own agenda.

entries.result: A merchant caravan whose lead wagon has a broken axle. The merchants are calm. The guards are watching the treeline.
A shrine at a crossroads, freshly tended, with an offering bowl that has something in it that was left within the last hour.
A royal courier who refuses to slow down, carrying the sealed seal of a court the party may or may not have reason to care about.
A stretch of road where the trees on both sides lean inward over the path, their branches touching, creating a tunnel that is beautiful and that birds avoid.
A patrol of soldiers with the colors of a lord the party hasn't heard of, asking where travelers are coming from and writing down the answers.
A wandering herbalist with a mule cart of samples who will trade remedies for news of any unusual plants seen along the road.
Smoke rising from a farmstead visible from the road — not cooking fire smoke — with no sound of alarm.
A mile marker that has been defaced and repainted, pointing now in a direction that is not the original.
A group of pilgrims in travel-worn robes moving in the same direction, singing quietly. They nod to the party but don't stop.
A tollgate with two guards and a fee. The gate is new. The guards are new. The legal authority behind the toll is unclear.
An abandoned camp — still warm fire, packs left behind — where travelers have left in a hurry and in one direction that leads off the road.
A horse, saddled and alone, standing at the road's edge, reins trailing. It is calm. It follows the party if not restrained.
A bard walking the road alone, collecting songs from travelers in exchange for performing them, who has an unusually specific question for anyone heading to the next city.
A section of road where the stones are old — far older than the current kingdom — and bear carvings that no one has translated.
A farmer's family blocking the road with a loaded wagon, trying to reach a relative in the next town before something catches them.
An old watchtower, crumbling, with a fire lit in its top story. Someone has been there recently enough that the fire is still burning.
A traveling magistrate hearing a dispute between two villagers at the roadside, in the open, with a small crowd of witnesses.
A section of road flooded by a creek that has jumped its bank. The water is knee-deep. Something is moving in it that isn't the current.
A fugitive who stops the party with a story that is probably true and a request for help that is very specific.
A road split not on any map, both paths equally traveled, with no sign indicating which leads to the next settlement.
A pair of travelers from a distant land who are very lost and very polite about it and who are carrying something valuable that they don't know is valuable.
A dead giant on the road — not recently dead, but not old either — that no one has moved and that is too large for the party to move without equipment.
A section of the road where the party's shadows point in the wrong direction regardless of the sun's position.
A wandering knight in armor showing damage that suggests a recent and significant fight, who will not say what she fought or where.
A tinker with a cart of tools, maps, and various useful items who has been on this road for thirty years and knows every family along it by name.
A message pinned to a tree, addressed to a specific name. The name belongs to someone in the party.
A road-side inn, small and well-maintained, whose host seems to know a great deal about travelers who passed through several days ago.
A group of mercenaries traveling without a contract, heading somewhere on their own account, who are friendly in the way that armed professionals are friendly when they don't need anything.
A section of road where it has snowed, or rained, or been sunny — weather that contradicts what's been true for the past hour of travel.
A traveling circus, much smaller than expected, with three performers who are considerably more skilled than a traveling circus typically produces.
An old woman sitting at the roadside who has been there long enough that birds have landed on her. She has a question. It is not a friendly question.
A patrol that is looking for someone and whose description of the person they're looking for is uncomfortably close to a description of someone in the party.
A bridge that is perfectly functional and guarded by something that has been there longer than the bridge and is not interested in tolls.
A burning wagon in the ditch, recently, with no one nearby and an intact strongbox in the wreckage.
A trader whose wagon is sealed and who will not discuss its contents but whose cargo moves occasionally despite the wagon being still.
A stretch of road where every birdsong is an imitation — the source is not a bird — and the imitations are just slightly wrong.
A family who left their village voluntarily — not running, just done — and are looking for somewhere to start over, asking travelers what's ahead.
A gravestone at the roadside, well-maintained, with a name and an epitaph that is too specific to be coincidental.
A road through a village where every door is closed and every window is shuttered in the middle of the day, and no one has said why.
A cart driver who is clearly hauling something illegal and is operating under the assumption that no one will ask if he maintains sufficient eye contact.
A map nailed to a post showing a location that the party's existing maps don't include, in apparent good faith.
A section of road where the party's equipment behaves strangely — compasses spin, torches dim, magical items feel wrong — for about two hundred paces.
A squad of soldiers escorting a prisoner who clearly doesn't belong in irons and who makes eye contact with the party in a way that asks a specific question.
An ambush that has been prepared for someone and triggered by the party, who are clearly not the intended targets.
A traveling healer who has been walking the same circuit for decades, known to every village on this road, whose advice about the current road conditions is always accurate.
A section of road where the party hears a bell — steady, deliberate — from no visible source, for as long as they remain in a specific stretch.
A checkpoint erected by a city that has no authority in this stretch of road, collecting 'road maintenance fees' and noting all travelers.
A blind beggar sitting at a crossroads who gives directions with absolute precision and then asks a question about the party's destination that suggests he already knows the answer.
A herd of livestock crossing the road being driven by farmhands who are underpaid and uninterested in moving faster than the animals feel like moving.
A wizard traveling with a cart full of equipment who is clearly mid-experiment and whose roadside camp, about a mile ahead, is producing visible effects on the sky.
A fresh landslide across the road that will take half a day to clear if started immediately, with a detour that adds two days.
A child running toward the party from a farm, out of breath, with a message that was supposed to be delivered an hour ago.
A diplomatic party traveling in the opposite direction with flags of three different nations, which is not a standard diplomatic configuration.
A roadside shrine where the offerings have been taken — not vandalized, taken carefully — within the past day.
A village that is celebrating something with the particular energy of people who have decided to celebrate right now regardless of circumstances.
A section of road overgrown with plants that are not native to this region, healthy, spreading, and flowering out of season.
A message scratched into the road surface itself, in a language the party can identify but not read.
A pair of monks traveling to a monastery they describe in detail, at a location that should be occupied by a forest.
A body at the road's edge, recently dead, with a sealed letter in the coat addressed to someone by name.
A stretch of road that is clearly much older than the road around it — finer stonework, different style — as if the newer road was built to connect to a section that was already there.
A merchant selling items that he claims were 'found' in a ruin two days' travel away, at prices that suggest he wants them gone more than he wants fair value.
A section of road where every traveler who passes has left a mark — carved into a stone, tied to a branch — a habit of this road that locals observe without explanation.
A retired soldier farming a roadside plot who will offer the party a meal in exchange for news of the wider world, and whose assessment of that news is sharper than expected.
A road agent operating a 'toll' by herself with a crossbow and a confidence that suggests she's done this many times.
A pack of wolves flanking the road at a distance, not threatening, apparently following the party in the same direction for reasons that become relevant later.
A ruined waystation with a working well, used as a campsite by travelers, with a logbook inside where people have been writing entries for twenty years.
A traveling physician who has collected a medicine that is available only in a specific distant location and is being paid very well to get it somewhere quickly.
A group of refugees who have been on the road for two weeks and have settled into a routine that is either functional or is a community slowly stopping.
A section of road where the party's footsteps echo incorrectly — too loud, too hollow — suggesting something below the road surface.
A knight's heraldry painted on a rock at the road's edge, marking the boundary of a claim. The heraldry belongs to a house that ended three generations ago.
A traveling teacher with a small group of students who asks the party three questions before allowing them to pass, on the grounds that the road is a classroom for those who use it.
An abandoned military camp — tents still standing, fires cold — that was occupied days ago and left in what appears to have been an orderly manner.
A funeral procession moving in the same direction, silent, with mourners who accept the party's quiet presence without comment.
A section of road where it smells, briefly and powerfully, like a specific thing — bread, the sea, woodsmoke — that has no source.
A tollkeeper whose records show that a specific person passed through three days ago and who will share this for a small consideration.
A wandering monster — large, dangerous — that is clearly injured and heading somewhere specific, not interested in the party unless provoked.
A pair of investigators from a guild the party has heard of, moving in the opposite direction, who ask carefully worded questions about conditions on the road ahead.
A child's doll hanging from a branch over the road — placed there deliberately, recently — which is someone's marker for something.
A road that visibly ends in a wall of fog. The fog is not natural. The road continues on the other side. What's in the fog is not discussed by locals.
A traveling fortune teller who has set up at a crossroads and whose predictions, observed by the party, have been accurate for the travelers who have already consulted her.
A message carved into a milestone: coordinates, a date, and a name. The date is tomorrow.
A festival crowd moving toward a celebration in a nearby town who will carry the party along with them whether or not the party wishes to attend.
A relay rider changing horses at a roadside stable who carries news from the capital that is two weeks old and was alarming two weeks ago.
A stretch of road where the trees have been systematically marked — not blazed for logging, something else — with a symbol that repeats at regular intervals.
A traveling judge riding circuit whose presence on this road means someone along it has a case pending.
A man walking the road who stops, listens, and then walks off the road at a right angle without comment, into open countryside, as if he heard something.
A section of road where all vegetation within ten feet on both sides is dead — not burned, not cut — and has been for some time.
A bounty hunter traveling in the same direction who makes conversation easily and is looking for someone whose description is in her notes.
A village in which every adult is armed — not unusually armed, just armed — going about normal business, and no one has offered a reason.
A road construction crew filling potholes who have found something under the road and are having a loud argument about what to do with it.
An old tower beside the road that has a light in its highest window and a locked door at its base and no visible means of access between the two.
A merchant who has been robbed, is furious, knows exactly who did it, and wants someone to deliver a very specific message to them.
A section of road where it rains on one side and does not rain on the other, with a clear dividing line that does not move.
A hermit who has been living beside this road for forty years, knows every regular traveler, and has a very specific question about someone who passed through last week.
A cart abandoned in a ditch, full of identical objects, none of which appear valuable, some of which hum faintly.
A young noble traveling alone for the first time, clearly, who is trying to appear experienced and needs help with something they won't ask for.
A section of road that the party is certain they have traveled before — same trees, same stones, same bend — but their maps say they haven't.
A traveler going the opposite direction who stops, looks at the party carefully, says 'don't take the left fork,' and continues without further explanation.
A caravan that has been here for days — no broken wheels, no sick animals — simply stopped. The merchants are waiting for something they are polite about not specifying.
The road ahead is empty in every direction the party can see, which has not happened once in the past three days of travel, and the silence that accompanies it has the quality of a held breath.
